skill.name: docstring
skill.description: Write PyTorch docstrings for functions and methods using PyTorch documentation conventions.
skill.summary: Generate PyTorch-style Sphinx docstrings with signatures, Args sections, examples, and cross-references.
skill.author: pytorch

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported weak cryptography, reconnaissance, command execution, and network indicators. Review found these are false positives from Markdown text, inline backticks, Python docstring examples, Sphinx roles, and paper reference URLs; no executable skill code or prompt injection was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown inline code, fenced Python examples, Sphinx roles, and docstring formatting guidance. These lines do not instruct the agent to execute shell commands and contain no script entry point.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Static patterns were present, but line context shows Markdown documentation examples rather than executable skill logic.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Terms in Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings are triggered by ordinary words and example identifiers inside documentation guidance. No hashing, encryption, signing, random generation, or cryptographic API usage appears in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows descriptive docstring content and a generated audit summary, not cryptographic implementation. I found no executable code path using a weak algorithm.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reference URLs Reported as Network Access
low_findings.description: The network findings are static links to an arXiv paper inside example documentation. They are not fetch calls, download commands, telemetry endpoints, or instructions to send data externally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only URLs are visible citation links in Sphinx reference examples. No network-capable code or command is present near these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in Benign Context
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings are caused by text about file paths, tensor data, and documentation references. The skill does not ask for system enumeration, network scanning, host discovery, or environment inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are documentation prose or generated analyzer output. No commands or procedural steps perform system or network reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: PyTorch documentation needs consistent signatures, Args sections, examples, and cross-references. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to produce docstrings that follow PyTorch conventions.
actual_capabilities: Structures signatures, brief descriptions, Args, Returns, and Examples sections.
Uses Sphinx roles for classes, functions, methods, attributes, math, and references.
Adds LaTeX-style tensor shapes and formulas where they are relevant.
Handles common PyTorch patterns such as aliases, in-place methods, and C-bound functions.
Provides checklist guidance for formatting, indentation, defaults, and examples.
limitations: It is tailored to PyTorch conventions, not every Python documentation style.
It does not execute examples or verify tensor shapes automatically.
It depends on the user to provide accurate function behavior and parameter details.
It does not build or publish generated documentation sites.

use_cases.title: Document a new PyTorch operator
use_cases.description: Create a first draft docstring with signature, arguments, return value, examples, and related API links.

use_cases.title: Standardize extension library docs
use_cases.description: Apply PyTorch documentation patterns to functions in a library that extends torch or torch.nn.

use_cases.title: Review documentation before a pull request
use_cases.description: Check a docstring for missing defaults, weak examples, incorrect Sphinx roles, and unclear tensor shape notation.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Docstring
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for a small tensor function.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a PyTorch-style docstring for this function signature. Include a brief description, Args section, Returns section, and one Examples block: [paste signature and behavior].

prompt_templates.title: Add Shape and Type Details
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate task for functions with tensor dimensions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve this PyTorch docstring by adding tensor shape notation, parameter types, default values, and a concise example: [paste current docstring].

prompt_templates.title: Document Related APIs
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate task for linking modules and functions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a PyTorch docstring that uses Sphinx cross-references to related classes, functions, methods, and attributes. Use this behavior summary: [paste details].

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a C-Bound Function Docstring
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced task for add_docstr or generated bindings.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an _add_docstr-style PyTorch docstring for a C-bound function. Include signature, Args, warnings if needed, examples, and external references: [paste API details].

output_examples.input: A contributor asks for a docstring for a tensor normalization function.
output: A raw string docstring with the function signature on the first line.
A concise description of the normalization behavior.
Args and Returns sections with tensor types, defaults, and shape notes.
An Examples block that demonstrates typical torch usage.

output_examples.input: A maintainer asks to review an existing docstring for PyTorch style.
output: A list of missing defaults, unclear parameter descriptions, and weak examples.
Suggested Sphinx cross-references for related torch APIs.
A revised docstring outline that keeps the original behavior intact.

best_practices: Provide the exact signature, parameter meanings, defaults, tensor shapes, and return behavior.
Ask for Sphinx roles and examples when the function relates to existing PyTorch APIs.
Review generated examples for correctness before adding them to production documentation.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to document unrelated APIs without adapting the conventions.
Do not omit defaults or optional markers for optional parameters.
Do not publish generated examples without checking that they run and match the function behavior.

faq.question: What documentation style does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses PyTorch Sphinx and reStructuredText conventions, including raw strings, Args sections, examples, and cross-references.

faq.question: Can it document C-bound PyTorch functions?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide includes patterns for _add_docstr and add_docstr_all documentation blocks.

faq.question: Does it test generated examples?
faq.answer: No. It writes documentation guidance and examples, but users should run or review examples separately.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. The skill is a Markdown guide and does not make network requests.

faq.question: Can it use mathematical formulas?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to use Sphinx math directives and inline math for formulas and tensor shapes.

faq.question: Is it useful outside PyTorch?
faq.answer: It can inspire similar documentation, but its rules are optimized for PyTorch project conventions.
